Tafseer:
Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth glorifies Allah. To Him belongs dominion, and to Him belongs praise, and He is over all things competent) (1).
(This entire universe invites to the worship of God): This verse has appeared in previous chapters with minor changes in wording, and we have already explained its various aspects. Here, it points to the reality that everything in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, teaching humans through its actions that the sovereignty of this universe belongs to Allah alone and that He alone is worthy of gratitude. No one is a partner or associate in His kingdom, nor is anyone else entitled to the gratitude of His servants.
(God has no partner): (And He is over all things competent) - It is also clear that He is powerful over all things. He is not in need of anyone's help in any of His works, no matter how great; therefore, associating partners with Him is completely meaningless. He did not seek anyone's help in creating the universe, nor did He need anyone in managing its affairs; rather, He did everything on His own, and just as He did before, He will continue to do in the future. For this reason, He alone is sufficient for His servants' trust. They should rely on Him alone and associate no one in His worship.

(It is He Who created you, and among you is the disbeliever, and among you is the believer. And Allah is Seeing of what you do) (2).
(It is inevitable for them to face the consequences of the misuse of their choice): That is, the God Whom the entire universe glorifies also created you; therefore, it was your duty to glorify Him just as all the stars in the heavens, the birds in the air, and the trees and stones on earth do. However, God has blessed you with choice, and as a result, there are disbelievers among you and believers as well. Thus, Allah Almighty sees your actions and will deal with each according to his deeds. If He did not do so, it would mean that disbelief and belief are equal in His sight, which is contrary to God's justice and wisdom.

(He created the heavens and the earth in truth and formed you and perfected your forms, and to Him is the destination) (3).
(Recompense is an essential requirement of divine providence): This is the proof for the preceding statement, showing that Allah Almighty created this world with a purposeful truth. The requirement of this purposeful truth is that there must inevitably come a Day when the lovers of truth receive the reward for their commitment, and those whose lives were spent contrary to this truth face its punishment.
(and formed you and perfected your forms): This draws attention to the care the Creator exercised in your creation. Anyone can see that human beings were created in the best mold, as stated in Surah At-Tin: (We have certainly created man in the best of molds) (95:4). The physical and internal structure of human beings, and the faculties and abilities bestowed upon them, bear clear witness that among all creations in this world, human beings hold the primary purpose. They are the crowning glory of creation; everything else is directly or indirectly meant for their service and benefit.
This level of care, and equipping human beings with superior external and internal faculties, testifies that the Creator did not produce them purposelessly or in vain, merely to eat, drink, and perish one day. If that were the case, all the care that nature spent on their creation and sustenance would be meaningless. Based on this very principle, the Qur'an repeatedly reminds human beings that the care Allah showed for your sustenance, the pure table of blessings He spread for you, and the distinction He gave you in shape and form among all His creations require that you be brought before Him one day to be questioned about the blessings He bestowed upon you.
(Allah is He who made for you the earth a place of settlement and the sky a ceiling and formed you and perfected your forms and provided you with good things...) (Al-Mu'min, 40:64).
The point is that this arrangement of good forms along with pure sustenance and a grand dwelling place is a clear proof that you are accountable before your Lord. Based on this very argument, those who denied the Hereafter and recompense were also warned: (O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous, Who created you, proportioned you, and balanced you? In whatever form He willed has He assembled you.) (Al-Infitar, 82:6-8).
This verse also clarifies the care that Allah Almighty exercised in creating human beings, while pointing in a highly serious tone to the responsibility that devolves upon them. (and to Him is the destination): That is, the God Who created this universe with a great purpose in view and gave you existence with such care will inevitably bring you back to Him one day for recompense. If this were not to happen, all of this arrangement would become meaningless.

(He knows what is within the heavens and the earth and knows what you conceal and what you declare. And Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts) (4).
(Nothing is outside the knowledge of Allah): That is, do not remain under the delusion that Allah Almighty would somehow remain unaware of all the secret and open actions of the entire world, and that He would not be able to account for everyone and recompense them. He is fully aware of whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and whether you perform a deed secretly or openly, He knows your every word and action; indeed, He is fully aware even of what is hidden in your breasts.

(Has there not come to you the news of those who disbelieved before? So they tasted the bad consequence of their affair, and they will have a painful punishment) (5).
(Historical testimony of the law of recompense): It is stated: Has the history of the past nations of this land not come to your knowledge, showing how they chose disbelief, and as a result, had to taste its evil consequence in this world, while a painful punishment awaits them in the Hereafter? This refers to Aad, Thamud, the people of Madyan, the people of Lot, and others, whose accounts have been detailed in the previous Surahs. The Quraysh were generally familiar with them, and they even had opportunities to pass by the ruins of some of these nations' settlements during their travels. Pointing to their history, they are warned that these events prove that Allah Almighty is not unconcerned with the good and evil of this world; rather, He constantly sent His messengers for its reform, and when those nations rejected the messengers, He punished them in a highly exemplary manner. The point is that when you see these examples of Allah's law of retribution with your own eyes on this earth, why do you find it unlikely that He will bring a Day when He will judge the entire world, manifesting His perfect justice and perfect mercy?

(That is because their messengers used to come to them with clear proofs, but they said, 'Shall human beings guide us?' and disbelieved and turned away. And Allah dispensed with them; and Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy) (6).
(The excuse of the deniers for rejecting the messengers): This explains why these nations fell under the grasp of Allah's punishment. It is stated: Allah's messengers came to them with clear signs and proofs for their guidance, but out of rebelliousness, they did not give them any weight. They fabricated the excuse: 'If Allah had to send a messenger for our guidance, He would have sent some superior creation. What is the meaning of sending humans like us as messengers? Are we so low that humans just like us will guide us?' The implication of their excuse was: 'If humans can guide us, then we are not inferior; we can guide ourselves, so why should we remain indebted to others?'
(Divine way regarding the deniers): (and disbelieved and turned away. And Allah dispensed with them) - That is, by raising such objections and excuses, they rejected the messenger and turned away from the call of truth, as a result of which Allah also became indifferent to them. Allah's established way is that He makes arrangements to bless His servants with guidance, but when people do not appreciate it, He leaves them to face the consequences of their ungratefulness. (And Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy) - It is stated that if Allah desires guidance for people, it is not because it brings Him any benefit. He is entirely independent of people's guidance or misguidance, and is inherently self-sufficient and perfect. He arranges guidance solely because the welfare of the people lies in it, but when they do not appreciate it, He does not force it upon them.

(Those who disbelieve have claimed that they will never be resurrected. Say, 'Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected; then you will surely be informed of what you did. And that, for Allah, is easy') (7).
(A decisive response): That is, these people claim that they will never be resurrected after death, and for this reason, they do not give any weight to the call of the prophets. (Say, 'Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected') - It is commanded: answer them with an oath and the same level of emphasis and certainty with which they deny resurrection after death, saying: Yes, by my Lord, you will surely be resurrected!
(The element of proof within the oath): Although the aspect of proof is not explicitly prominent in this phrase—as the claim is answered with a claim because it is said in response to the baseless claim of the deniers—a subtle proof is implied in the oath 'by my Lord'. This is because Allah's lordship (Rububiyyah), the manifestations of which are evident in every corner of this universe, requires that He will not treat the righteous and the wicked in the same manner. He must inevitably reward the righteous for their goodness and punish the wicked for their evil. This requirement makes it necessary that He resurrects people after death, takes their account, and recompenses them according to their deeds.
(And that, for Allah, is easy): That is, do not consider this task impossible or difficult by comparing Allah's power with your own. For others, this task is indeed impossible, as their knowledge and power are highly limited; but for Allah, it is extremely easy. He is the possessor of unlimited knowledge and unlimited power.

(So believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Light which We have sent down. And Allah is Acquainted with what you do) (8).
(A powerful warning to accept faith): This is a powerful warning that serves as an invitation to faith. That is, do not reject the Messenger by making such excuses, but believe in Allah, His Messenger, and the Light He has sent down. The 'Light' refers to the Holy Qur'an, which Allah Almighty revealed to distinguish between truth and falsehood. It should be noted that the light Allah had given to the People of the Book to distinguish between guidance and deviation was lost by them—as detailed in the previous Surahs—rendering them unable to distinguish between truth and falsehood. However, when He revealed His light anew in its perfect form to guide mankind, instead of appreciating it, they exerted all their efforts to extinguish it with their mouths, neither benefiting from it themselves nor letting others benefit.
(They want to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light, although the disbelievers dislike it) (As-Saff, 61:8).
(And Allah is Acquainted with what you do): That is, do not remain under the delusion that Allah is unaware of your deeds. Whatever you are doing is in His knowledge, and one day it will all confront you.

(The Day He will assemble you for the Day of Assembly—that is the Day of Deprivation [and gain]. And whoever believes in Allah and does righteousness—He will remove from him his misdeeds and admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they will abide forever. That is the great attainment) (9).
(The path of safety): That is, instead of keeping yourselves in the delusion that Allah cannot bring you back to life after death, safety lies in constantly remembering the Day when Allah will assemble you for the Day of Assembly—the Day that is fixed in Allah's plan, the proofs of which are present in the universe and within yourselves, to which all prophets, messengers, and heavenly books have borne witness, the occurrence of which is necessary for this world to have purpose, and without which this world would remain vain and a mere plaything.
In another place, He says: (That is a Day for which the people will be collected, and that is a Day [which will be] witnessed) (Hud, 11:103). Elsewhere, He says: (Say, 'Indeed, the former and the latter generations will surely be gathered for the appointment of a known Day') (Al-Waqi'ah, 56:49-50). The emphasis in these verses on gathering all the earlier and later generations at a fixed time is to dispel the doubts of those foolish people who think: who could possibly collect such countless creations from every corner of land and sea, rivers and mountains? It is stated that Allah has fixed a Day for this assembly, and it is bound to happen. Instead of remaining in doubts, prepare for it and keep it in mind.
(The Day of loss and gain): (that is the Day of Deprivation) - Shah Abdul Qadir translated 'At-Taghabun' as 'the day of winning and losing'. In our view, this translation perfectly captures the spirit of the word. The Qur'an itself explains this winning and losing in the subsequent part, stating: those who believe and do righteous deeds, Allah will cleanse them of the effects of their errors and admit them into gardens beneath which rivers flow, where they will abide forever; and those who achieve this have indeed achieved a great victory. On the other hand, those who disbelieved and rejected His signs will be cast into the Fire, abiding therein forever—a wretched destination.
The point is that those who deny the Hereafter consider this world as the only arena for winning and losing. Those who achieve material prosperity in this world assume they have won the game, while those who remain deprived of it are considered failures. However, this world is not the home of reward, but the home of trial. The home of reward is the Hereafter, where the game will be won by those who lead a life of faith and righteous deeds in this world, even if they received none of the world's goods; and those who rise without faith and righteous deeds will be completely ruined and bankrupt there, even if they possessed the treasures of Qarun in this world.
It should be noted that for those who consider this world as the only arena for winning and losing, it is impossible to sacrifice their comfort and spend their wealth to help and serve others. Even if they occasionally feel inclined to do so, they are immediately discouraged by the fear: 'What if some unforeseen calamity or difficulty arises tomorrow?' However, for the one who regards the Hereafter as the true arena for winning and losing, no such fear can discourage him. If any such doubt arises in his heart, he recognizes it as a satanic whisper and boldly risks his resources for the success of his Hereafter, relying on his Lord.

(But those who disbelieved and denied Our signs—those are the companions of the Fire, abiding eternally therein; and wretched is the destination) (10).
[This entry corresponds to the latter part of the translation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer of verses 9 and 10 was combined in the original text.]

(No disaster strikes except by permission of Allah. And whoever believes in Allah—He will guide his heart. And Allah is Knowing of all things) (11).
(Remedy for a whisper of doubt): This dispels the doubt that can hinder one from striving for success in the Hereafter. It is stated: as you take steps in the path of obedience to Allah and His Messenger and in seeking His pleasure, do not give any weight to the whisper of doubt: 'What if some difficulty arises tomorrow?' No calamity can befall a servant except by the permission of Allah. This is a reality that no believer in Allah can deny. Therefore, the servant should be reassured that any task performed in obedience to Allah's command cannot become a source of trial beyond his endurance. Allah is most knowing of the strength and capabilities of His servants, and He does not burden any soul beyond its capacity.
(And whoever believes in Allah—He will guide his heart. And Allah is Knowing of all things) - This comforts those who face trials in the path of Allah: if you remain firm in your faith, no trial will be able to discourage you; rather, Allah will guide your heart through the Holy Spirit at the critical moment. Your Lord is aware of all things; He knows the state of His servants and arranges help from the unseen at the right time. This is also what Jesus (peace be upon him) said to his disciples: when people hand you over to the courts for my sake, do not worry about what to say, for my Lord will help you through the Holy Spirit at that very moment. This theme has also appeared in Surah Al-Hadid, verse 22; refer to it for details.

(And obey Allah and obey the Messenger; but if you turn away—then upon Our Messenger is only [the duty of] clear notification) (12).
That is, do not shrink from obeying Allah and His Messenger out of baseless anxieties and doubts. Otherwise, remember that the Messenger's only responsibility is to convey Allah's commands and guidance clearly. Once he has fulfilled this duty, he is absolved of responsibility before Allah. You will not be questioned about his message, but you will be questioned about why you did not accept his invitation.

(Allah—there is no deity except Him. And upon Allah let the believers rely) (13).
It is stated that there is no other deity in the heavens or the earth from whom any harm is to be feared or any benefit is to be expected. Allah alone is the One Who can bring benefit or harm. The believers should rely on Allah alone; this is the very requirement of their faith.

(O you who have believed, indeed, among your wives and your children are enemies to you, so beware of them. But if you pardon and overlook and forgive—then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) (14).
(Warning against a major trial): This warns against one of the greatest trials that hinders a person from fulfilling Allah's rights and duties and from spending their life and wealth in His cause—namely, the love of wives and children. This love is a natural thing, but at the same time, it is a major trial for human beings. This love and the love of God often clash. If a person's knowledge and faith are not deep, there is a risk that the interests of his wives and children and his love for them may overwhelm him to the extent that he ignores the love of God, which can ruin his faith. Arab poets often mention the blame of those who discourage them from sacrifice and generosity, and a Hadith states that a person's wives and children are the primary causes of his miserliness and cowardice. This verse points to this reality: some of a person's family members act as his enemies by holding him back from fulfilling Allah's rights. Although they may do this out of apparent goodwill, it leads to ultimate ruin; hence, they are actually doing what an enemy does.
In 'indeed, among your wives', the particle 'min' denotes partitives (tab'eed), implying that it is not necessary that every person's wives and children be like this. Many have families that assist them in the path of truth instead of hindering them. However, if someone's family is not of this supportive nature, he should beware of them lest they become a trap for him.
(But if you pardon and overlook and forgive—then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) - This explains the manner of being wary: while it is necessary to ensure they do not hold you back from the path of Allah, you must also ensure that, as far as possible, this does not lead to severing ties or separation. Practice pardon, tolerance, and forgiveness as much as possible, and hope that Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. He will overlook your shortcomings and forgive their weaknesses.
Thus, for one who faces such a trial, the correct approach is to protect himself from falling into fitnah (trial) and try to reform the weaknesses of his family through his words and actions. However, as long as it does not involve a conflict between belief and disbelief, he should not sever ties with them, but should practice pardon and forgiveness. He should live with them, but with care and caution, protecting himself while working to reform them.

(Your wealth and your children are only a trial, and Allah has with Him a great reward) (15).
This further clarifies the subject. 'Fitnah' means trial and examination. It is stated: your wealth and your children are your trial. Allah has tested you through them to see whether you become entangled in their love and forget God and His rights, or make them a means to attain God's love and pleasure. If you choose the first path, it means you failed God's test. You prioritized the love of wealth and children over the love of God, whereas faith requires that a person love Allah more than anything else: (And those who believe are stronger in love for Allah) (Al-Baqarah, 2:165). If you choose the second path, it is the path of success and salvation. If you suffer any loss in this world by choosing this path, be assured that Allah Almighty will compensate you for every loss with a great reward in the Hereafter.

(So fear Allah as much as you are able and listen and obey and spend; [that is] better for your souls. And whoever is protected from the covetousness of his soul—it is those who will be the successful) (16).
(The path of success): This is the final advice in this regard: fear Allah constantly lest Satan leads you into some trial. This fear should be to the utmost of your ability. If you try to fear Allah to the extent of your capacity, Allah Almighty will not allow Satan to overcome you; otherwise, by falling into some trial of wealth and children, you may drift so far from Allah's path that no possibility of return remains for you.
(and listen and obey and spend; [that is] better for your souls) - This states the same point in a positive manner: listen to and obey Allah and His Messenger, and respond to the call to spend in the cause of God. The primary benefit of this spending will not go to Allah or His Messenger, but to yourselves, if you spend with sincerity and generosity.
(And whoever is protected from the covetousness of his soul—it is those who will be the successful) - 'Shuhh' means stinginess and greed. It is stated: the stinginess that lies within the human soul, if it overcomes the soul, becomes a cause of ruin. Blessed are those servants of Allah who are protected from its dominance; they are the ones who will succeed in the Hereafter!
The attribution of 'shuhh' (stinginess) to the soul (nafs) proves that among the inclinations of which the human soul is composed, stinginess also has its place. However, it is one of those inclinations which, if not guarded against, can lead to human ruin, just like lust or anger. Therefore, it is necessary not to give it so much rein that it dominates the soul and suppresses the feelings of sacrifice and generosity. The way to achieve this is that when this inclination begins to dominate, a person should suppress it by spending in charity contrary to its demands, until it becomes weak enough not to hinder acts of goodness. The wording of the Qur'an indicates that those who try to suppress this inclination succeed in their effort by the grace of Allah, and those who succeed in this effort are the ones entitled to success in the Hereafter. This is because among the deeds of seeking Allah's pleasure, spending in His cause holds the highest rank, especially the spending that a person does by disregarding his own personal needs: (and give them preference over themselves, even though they are in privation) (Al-Hashr, 59:9).

(If you loan Allah a goodly loan, He will multiply it for you and forgive you. And Allah is Appreciative and Forbearing) (17).
(The occasion and reality of the goodly loan): This mentions a specific type after the general command. The spending commanded above applies to all kinds of spending in the cause of Allah, whether related to charity, zakat, or jihad. This verse specifically emphasizes spending for jihad. In the Qur'an, the term 'loan' (qard) usually refers to spending for jihad, and the appeal and eloquence in using this word require no explanation. In Surah Al-Muzzammil, He says:
(And establish prayer and give zakat and loan Allah a goodly loan) (Al-Muzzammil, 73:20).
In this verse, 'and loan Allah' mentioned after 'and give zakat' refers to the spending required in the cause of Allah under specific circumstances.
(He will multiply it for you and forgive you) - 'muda'afah' (multiplying) does not merely mean doubling, but also carries the meaning of increasing generally, whether it is in the form of doubling or multiplying manifold. We have discussed this in its relevant place, and here it is used in the same sense. The meaning is: when Allah asks you for a loan, do not fall into the misunderstanding that His treasures have decreased, prompting Him to ask for a loan. His treasures remain full as ever. He is asking for this loan solely to open the way of profit for you, so that by spending one unit in His cause, you may receive ten or even seventy times more in the Hereafter. The only condition for this is that it must be a 'goodly loan' (qard hasan). We have explained in its relevant place that a 'goodly loan' is one given from good wealth, given with a cheerful heart and generosity, and given despite one's own need. Allah will multiply the loan that possesses these qualities many times over, return it to those who gave it, and bless them with His forgiveness.
(Reference to Allah's attributes): (And Allah is Appreciative and Forbearing) - 'Shakur' means the One Who accepts with appreciation. That is, Allah Almighty is highly appreciative of His servants' good deeds and welcomes them warmly. Although He is Free of need and Praiseworthy, and the offerings His servants present to Him are from the very wealth He gave them, He does not regard them as insignificant; rather, He accepts them with appreciation and nurtures them. Along with this, He is 'Halim' (Forbearing), and therefore deals with His servants with extreme generosity. If they avoid major sins, He overlooks their lapses and shortcomings.

(Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Exalted in Might, the Wise) (18).
That is, Allah is the Knower of all that is hidden and manifest, and is also All-Mighty and All-Wise. Whatever sacrifice you make to seek His pleasure will not remain hidden from Him, and be assured that if you support His cause, He is not a weak being but is dominant over all things, and His every act is based on wisdom. Those who rely on Him never fail, and those who follow His commands never stumble.
With the help of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. (All praise belongs to Him, and in His hand is all guidance). Rahmanabad, April 13, 1978 / Jumada al-Awwal 4, 1398 AH.
